summary: I was in my office when I first heard from the media that a plane had crashed into the World Trade Center in New York. Shortly after, we saw a second plane hit, and we immediately suspected a terrorist attack. This was the beginning of the 9/11 attacks, and it set off a chain of events that required us to mobilize quickly in Washington, D.C.

New York City was the initial site of the 9/11 attacks when the World Trade Center was struck by two planes. I was monitoring the events from Washington, D.C., as the news unfolded. The attacks in New York City were the catalyst for the heightened security and emergency response efforts we undertook in the capital.

While I was traveling downtown in Washington, I heard over the radio that a plane had struck the Pentagon. I saw the smoke from the freeway and knew the situation was serious. Our helicopters were among the first Medivac units on the scene, helping burn victims, and we deployed officers there to assist with traffic and perimeter control alongside the FBI and Arlington County police.

I was the acting Operations Division commander for the United States Park Police during the 9/11 attacks. I established command posts and deployed units to secure key locations like the White House and major monuments. We managed evacuations, handled suspicious packages, and coordinated with other agencies to maintain security throughout Washington, D.C.

During the 9/11 attacks, we deployed United States Park Police units around the White House to secure the area and evacuate high-level officials. We worked closely with the Secret Service to maintain security and manage evacuations of the President, Vice President, and Cabinet members. The White House area was a critical focus for our operations throughout the day and following days.

As part of the security measures during the 9/11 attacks, we deployed units to the Lincoln Memorial to begin evacuation procedures and secure the area. Along with other major monuments, it was important to protect these symbolic sites from any potential threats. We maintained a strong police presence there for several days following the attacks.

The Jefferson Memorial was one of the key sites we secured during the 9/11 attacks. We evacuated visitors and increased police presence to prevent any further threats. Our efforts ensured the safety of this important monument throughout the heightened alert period in Washington, D.C.

During the 9/11 attacks, the Washington Monument was among the main memorials we secured and evacuated. We deployed manpower to maintain security and worked long shifts to ensure continuous coverage. The monument was part of the broader effort to protect national landmarks in the National Capital Region.

The Secret Service was a key partner during the 9/11 attacks, coordinating closely with the United States Park Police to secure the White House and escort high-level officials. We worked together on evacuation plans and maintained command posts to manage the evolving threat. Their role was critical in protecting the President, Vice President, and Cabinet members throughout the crisis.

entity: Metropolitan Police Department of the District of Columbia
summary: The Metropolitan Police Department of the District of Columbia worked alongside the United States Park Police and Secret Service during the 9/11 attacks. We deployed command officials to their command post and coordinated evacuations and security measures downtown. Their support was vital in managing the large-scale emergency response in Washington, D.C.

The 9/11 attacks began with planes striking the World Trade Center in New York City, followed by the Pentagon in Washington, D.C., and a fourth plane crashing in Pennsylvania. I was deeply involved in the response as acting Operations Division commander for the United States Park Police, managing evacuations, security, and coordination with multiple agencies. The attacks caused widespread panic, traffic gridlock, and a massive security mobilization in the National Capital Region.

On the morning of 9/11, we implemented the evening PM rush hour traffic pattern on Rock Creek Parkway to help evacuate vehicles from downtown Washington more effectively. Traffic was gridlocked and people were panicking, so this change was critical to managing the flow and reducing congestion. It was part of our broader traffic control efforts during the crisis.

During the 9/11 attacks, we were heavily involved in evacuating members of the Cabinet from Washington, D.C. This included the Secretary of the Interior and her staff, among others. We coordinated closely with the Secret Service to ensure their safe relocation to secure locations amid the chaos.

entity: United States Secretary of the Interior
summary: The Secretary of the Interior and her staff were evacuated from Washington, D.C. during the 9/11 attacks as part of our efforts to protect high-level officials. We coordinated these movements carefully with the Secret Service and other agencies to ensure their safety. The Department of the Interior was also kept informed through their Watch Center during the crisis.

The National Park Service played a role during the 9/11 attacks, with their Director and staff being evacuated alongside other officials. We worked with the National Park Service Maintenance Division to set up lighting and fencing around monuments for better security. Their communication centers, including one at Shenandoah National Park, were kept updated on the situation in Washington.

During the 9/11 attacks, we provided escorts and security for the President of the United States as he was moved to various secure locations. The situation was fluid, and the President's safety was a top priority, requiring coordination with the Secret Service and other agencies. Our units helped facilitate quick and safe travel through the congested city.

entity: Vice President of the United States
summary: The Vice President of the United States was among the high-level officials we escorted and evacuated during the 9/11 attacks. Our police units worked closely with the Secret Service to ensure the Vice President's safe movement amid the chaos and heavy traffic. This was part of the broader effort to protect key government leaders during the crisis.

During the 9/11 attacks, we provided security escorts for the United States Secretary of State as part of the evacuation and protection efforts. The Secretary's safe movement through Washington was coordinated with the Secret Service and other law enforcement agencies. This was a critical part of our response to ensure continuity of government functions.

entity: United States Department of the Interior
summary: The Department of the Interior was actively involved during the 9/11 attacks through its Watch Center, which we kept in constant communication with. They monitored security concerns related to dams and other assets under their jurisdiction. The Secretary of the Interior and her staff were also evacuated from Washington as part of the emergency response.
cues: Department of the Interior Watch Center

summary: During the 9/11 attacks, a command communications center was set up at Shenandoah National Park to receive updates from Washington. Although no incidents occurred there, the center was kept informed as part of the National Park Service's broader security response. This helped ensure readiness across all National Park Service areas.

The National Capital Region was the focus of intense security operations during and after the 9/11 attacks. We secured major monuments, memorials, and federal buildings throughout the area, maintaining continuous police coverage. Traffic patterns were adjusted, and multiple command posts were established to coordinate the complex response.

Arlington County, Virginia, was involved in the response to the Pentagon attack on 9/11. We worked alongside Arlington County police to manage traffic and perimeter control at the Pentagon crime scene. Their cooperation was essential in supporting the FBI and other agencies during the emergency.

The FBI was heavily involved at the Pentagon crime scene following the 9/11 attacks. We assisted them with traffic and perimeter control as they conducted their investigation. Coordination with the FBI was a key part of the multi-agency response in Washington, D.C.

Lafayette Square was one of the areas we secured during the 9/11 attacks to prevent any potential threats near the White House. We maintained a strong police presence there along with other key locations like the Ellipse. Our efforts included fencing, lighting, and continuous patrols to ensure safety.

The Ellipse was another critical site we secured during the 9/11 attacks. Along with Lafayette Square and other monuments, we increased manpower and maintained 24-hour coverage to protect these areas. Our coordination with the National Park Service helped us manage lighting and fencing to enhance security.
